
# Exercise Sheet 1

::: {.wideblock}

## Part A

### Exercise 1 (Functions and Stored Procedures)


> The Great Outdoors retail chain wants to create a dashboard showing different aspects of their sales performance for analytics. They want to display the monthly sales performance of a given product for a specific year across all retailers in the dashboard. For this purpose, they need a `get_monthly_sales` function that given a `product_id` and a year, returns a table with 12 rows, showing the total sales (income) for each month of that `year`.


#### Problem 1


> Create a function that computes the total sales for all months as follows
>
> - For each month, run an aggregation query on `go_daily_sales` to calculate the total sales of that month.
> - If no sales exist for a month, output 0 as the total sales for that month.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION get_monthly_sales(product_id BIGINT, year BIGINT)
RETURNS TABLE (month BIGINT, total_sales DECIMAL)
AS $$
DECLARE
  n_month INT;
BEGIN
  FOR n_month IN 1..12 LOOP
    month := n_month;

    SELECT COALESCE(SUM(quantity * unit_sale_price), 0)
    INTO total_sales
    FROM go_daily_sales
    WHERE product_number = product_id 
      AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = year
      AND EXTRACT(MONTH FROM date) = n_month;

    RETURN NEXT;
  END LOOP;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
```
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Result"}
| month | total sales |
|:-----:|:-----------:|
|     1 |  753,928.75 |
|     2 |  602,276.00 |
|     3 |  502,065.25 |
|     4 |  535,228.00 |
|     5 |   64,880.50 |
|     6 |  627,852.50 |
|     7 |  320,139.75 |
|     8 |  495,635.00 |
|     9 |  638,834.50 |
|    10 |  711,211.72 |
|    11 |1,155,027.75 |
|    12 |  562,755.25 |

: Result of executing `SELECT * FROM get_monthly_sales(109110, 2016)` {.striped .hover}
:::

#### Problem 2

> Rewrite the function using a cursor to improve its efficiency.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION get_monthly_sales(product_id BIGINT, year INT)
RETURNS TABLE (month INT, total_sales DECIMAL)
AS $$
DECLARE
  n_month INT;
  sales DECIMAL[12] := ARRAY_FILL(0, ARRAY[12]);
  record RECORD;
  cursor CURSOR FOR
    SELECT EXTRACT(MONTH FROM date) AS month,
           SUM(quantity * unit_sale_price) AS total_sales
    FROM go_daily_sales
    WHERE product_number = product_id
      AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM date) = year
    GROUP BY month;

BEGIN
  OPEN cursor;
  LOOP
    FETCH cursor INTO record;
    EXIT WHEN NOT FOUND;
    sales[record.month] := record.total_sales;
  END LOOP;
  CLOSE cursor;

  FOR n_month in 1..12 LOOP
    month := n_month;
    total_sales := sales[n_month];
    RETURN NEXT;
  END LOOP;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
```
:::

#### Problem 3

> Discuss the difference in efficiency of the two approaches

The difference is significant (3 times!):

- ~4.2ms for the solution with loop,
- ~1.3ms for the solution with cursor.

(The runs did not include function declaration.)

With the increase of the data size, differences would obviously increase further. As each iteration of the loop processes the whole table, it is much less efficient than the solution with a cursor, which in our case processes the table only once.


### Exercise 2 (Triggers)

> GO retail chain wants to maintain a `last_sale_date` in the `go_products` table. After adding the column:
> ```sql
> ALTER TABLE go_products ADD COLUMN last_sale_date DATE;
> ```
> they decide to use triggers to enforce and manage updates. Create triggers for the following scenarios and show how each trigger is attached to its table.

#### Problem 1

> The GO team wants to ensure data integrity for the `last_sale_date` column. They need a row-level trigger that fires on every update of `last_sale_date` for a product, with the following behavior:
> 
> - If the new `last_sale_date` is earlier than the existing value, the trigger should raise an exception to prevent the update.
> - If the new date passes the validation, the trigger should log that the update was successful (Use `RAISE NOTICE`) and allow the update to proceed. Note that in the case where there is no previous date, this update should be allowed.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```sql
ALTER TABLE go_products ADD COLUMN last_sale_date DATE;

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION check_last_sale_date()
RETURNS TRIGGER
AS $$
  BEGIN
    IF NEW.last_sale_date < OLD.last_sale_date THEN
      RAISE EXCEPTION 'Error: It is wrong to update `last_sale_date` to an earlier date';
    END IF;

    RAISE NOTICE 'Update proceeded successfully';

    RETURN NEW;
  END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_last_sale_date
BEFORE UPDATE OF last_sale_date
ON go_products
FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION check_last_sale_date();
```
:::




#### Problem 2

> The GO team wants to automatically maintain the `last_sale_date` in `go_products` whenever new sales are recorded in `go_daily_sales`. Since sales are often inserted in bulk, multiple tuples may be added with a single `INSERT` statement. Create a statement-level trigger that fires once per `INSERT` statement on `go_daily_sales`. The trigger should, update the `last_sale_date` in `go_products` to the current date for all products that have new sales in the inserted rows and log the start and end of the execution of the trigger for auditing purposes (Use `RAISE NOTICE`).


::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```sql
ALTER TABLE go_products ADD COLUMN last_sale_date DATE;

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION update_last_sale_date()
RETURNS TRIGGER
AS $$
BEGIN
  RAISE NOTICE 'Trigger execution started';

  UPDATE go_products
  SET last_sale_date = CURRENT_DATE
  WHERE product_number IN (
    SELECT DISTINCT product_number
    FROM new_sales
  );

  RAISE NOTICE 'Trigger execution ended';

  RETURN NULL;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_update_last_sale_date
AFTER INSERT ON go_daily_sales
REFERENCING NEW TABLE AS new_sales
FOR EACH STATEMENT
EXECUTE FUNCTION update_last_sale_date();
```
:::





#### Problem 3

> Execute single inserts and bulk inserts into `go_daily_sales` and observe the behaviour of the triggers. Explain the behaviour in terms of which triggers fire, the order in which they execute, and how often each trigger fires.


::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Testing 2.1"}
```sql
UPDATE go_products
SET last_sale_date = '2026-01-02'
WHERE product_number = 1110;

UPDATE go_products
SET last_sale_date = '2026-01-03'
WHERE product_number = 1110;


UPDATE go_products
SET last_sale_date = '2026-01-01'
WHERE product_number = 1110;
```
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Tests' Outcomes (2.1)"}

**Statement 1** *(UPDATE go_products)*
```text
NOTICE: Update proceeded successfully
UPDATE executed
```

**Statement 2** *(UPDATE go_products)*
```text
NOTICE: Update proceeded successfully
UPDATE executed
```

```text
ERROR: Execution stopped here due to an error: It is wrong to update `last_sale_date` to an earlier date
```
:::


First two statements were correct and triggered the notice which announced the successful updates. The last statement attempted to update the `last_sale_date` to an earlier date which was caught by the trigger which prevented the update.

The second trigger was not invoked since these statements didn't fall into its conditions.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Testing 2.2"}
```sql
INSERT INTO go_daily_sales VALUES (1201, 1110, 1, '2026-01-04', 1, 1.0, 1.0);

INSERT INTO go_daily_sales VALUES
  (1202, 2110, 1, '2026-01-04', 1, 1.0, 1.0),
  (1201, 3110, 1, '2026-01-04', 1, 1.0, 1.0),
  (1201, 4110, 1, '2026-01-04', 1, 1.0, 1.0);

SELECT product_number, last_sale_date 
FROM go_products 
WHERE product_number IN (1110, 2110, 3110, 4110);
```
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Tests' Outcomes (2.2)"}

**Statement 1** *(INSERT INTO go_daily_sales VALUES)*
```text
NOTICE: Trigger execution started
NOTICE: Update proceeded successfully
NOTICE: Trigger execution ended
INSERT executed
```

**Statement 2** *(INSERT INTO go_daily_sales VALUES)*
```text
NOTICE: Trigger execution started
NOTICE: Update proceeded successfully
NOTICE: Update proceeded successfully
NOTICE: Update proceeded successfully
NOTICE: Trigger execution ended
INSERT executed
```

**Statement 3** *(SELECT product_number, last_sale_date)*

| # | product_number | last_sale_date |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 4110 | "2026-03-23T00:00:00.000Z" |
| 2 | 1110 | "2026-03-23T00:00:00.000Z" |
| 3 | 3110 | "2026-03-23T00:00:00.000Z" |
| 4 | 2110 | "2026-03-23T00:00:00.000Z" |

:::


Both update statements (Statements 1 and 2) were correct from the viewpoint of the trigger in 2.1. The first trigger output its message once for the first statement and thrice for the second statement (thrice, the first trigger is a statement-level: `FOR EACH ROW`).

Of course, the second trigger was also activated which is evident in the results: notices of each statement start and end with the notice by the trigger. Since second trigger updates the rows between its execution's logs, the first trigger's notices happen between the logs.


## Part B

### Exercise 3

The naive algorithm is to start with a set of facts and based on the rules deduce new facts in rounds until a round with no new rules. The algorithm is naive since it uses all facts in each round, even the pairs of old facts. This is wasteful because we already know the deduced facts for such pairs from the previous rounds.

In my semi-naive algorithm, I skip such pairs. In other words, at least one element in a pair must be a fact derived in the previous iteration.

The results are formatted as a kind of pseudocode.

#### Round 1

Initialisation. Every flight is a route.

```text
delta 1:
  VIE-BER, VIE-PAR, BER-AMS, PAR-AMS, PAR-BCN, AMS-BCN, AMS-OPO, BCN-OPO

route 1 = Delta 1 (8 routes)
```

#### Round 2

```text
  VIE-BER + BER-AMS = VIE-AMS
  VIE-PAR + PAR-AMS = VIE-AMS         # duplicate
  VIE-PAR + PAR-BCN = VIE-BCN
  BER-AMS + AMS-OPO = BER-OPO
  BER-AMS + AMS-BCN = BER-BCN
  PAR-AMS + AMS-OPO = PAR-OPO
  PAR-AMS + AMS-BCN = PAR-BCN        # already known
  PAR-BCN + BCN-OPO = PAR-OPO        # duplicate
  AMS-BCN + BCN-OPO = AMS-OPO        # already known

temp 2 = VIE-AMS, VIE-BCN, BER-BCN, BER-OPO, PAR-BCN, PAR-OPO, AMS-OPO

delta 2 = temp 2 minus route 1
        = VIE-AMS, VIE-BCN, BER-BCN, BER-OPO, PAR-OPO

route 2 = route 1 + Delta 2 (13 routes)
```

#### Round 3
```text
  VIE-AMS + AMS-BCN = VIE-BCN        # already known
  VIE-AMS + AMS-OPO = VIE-OPO        # new
  VIE-BCN + BCN-OPO = VIE-OPO        # duplicate
  BER-BCN + BCN-OPO = BER-OPO        # already known
  VIE-BER + BER-BCN = VIE-BCN        # already known
  VIE-BER + BER-OPO = VIE-OPO        # duplicate
  VIE-PAR + PAR-OPO = VIE-OPO        # duplicate

temp 3 = VIE-BCN, VIE-OPO, BER-OPO

delta 3 = temp 3 minus route 2
        = VIE-OPO

route 3 = route 2 + Delta 3 (14 routes)
```

#### Round 4
```text
OPO has no outgoing routes and no route ends at VIE.

temp 4 = empty
delta 4 = empty

Done.
```

#### The Final List

```text
VIE-BER, VIE-PAR, BER-AMS, PAR-AMS, PAR-BCN, AMS-BCN, BCN-OPO, AMS-OPO,
VIE-AMS, VIE-BCN, BER-BCN, BER-OPO, PAR-OPO, VIE-OPO
```

:::
